content_chunks.heading: Introduction to Modern History Sources
content_chunks.text: The history of India in the post-independence era is studied through various sources. These sources include written sources, material sources, oral sources, and sources in the audio-visual medium. Modern history sources differ from ancient and medieval sources as modern history sources are available at regional, state, national, and international levels.

content_chunks.heading: Written Sources: Newspapers and Periodicals
content_chunks.text: Written sources of history include newspapers, periodicals, diaries, reference books, correspondence, government gazettes, postage stamps, and documents in the archives. Newspapers are considered the fourth pillar of democracy in the modern period and serve as a major medium of information. Newspapers provide details on national and international affairs, politics, art, sports, literature, and social and cultural affairs. Most national newspapers have started regional editions and publish supplements that provide information on various specialized topics. Some newspapers produce special supplements at the end of the year that take an overview of the important events of that year.

content_chunks.heading: Press Trust of India (PTI)
content_chunks.text: After 1953, the Press Trust of India (PTI) became an important source of primary details for all important events and articles on important subjects. The Press Trust of India provides reports, photographs, and articles on financial and scientific issues to newspapers. During the 1990s, the Press Trust of India started using satellite broadcast technology instead of tele-printers to send news all over the country. This material is essential for writing the history of modern India.

content_chunks.heading: Postage Stamps and Jal Cooper
content_chunks.text: Postage stamps do not reveal information on their own, but a historian makes postage stamps speak. Since India became independent, there have been several changes in postage stamps regarding sizes, novelty in subjects, and colour schemes. The Postal Department issues stamps on themes like political leaders, flowers, animals, birds, and events like silver or golden jubilees. Jal Cooper was an internationally acclaimed philatelist and an expert on postage stamps. Jal Cooper founded the first Philatelic Bureau in India and the Empire of India Philatelic Society. The Indian government issued a postage stamp on Jal Cooper in 1977 to recognize Jal Cooper's significant contribution to the study of Indian postage stamps.

content_chunks.heading: Material Sources
content_chunks.text: Material sources include coins, places of worship, things in daily use, royal seals, ornaments, museums, clothing, and modern architecture. Coins help in understanding history through changes in printing currency notes and the metals used for making coins. Coins often convey messages like population control or the importance of agriculture. Museums in all states of India depict the characteristics and display the cultural and social heritage of the state, such as the Chhatrapati Shivaji Maharaj Museum in Mumbai. Some private collectors also set up museums based on distinctive subjects like coins, notes, and lamps.

content_chunks.heading: Oral Sources
content_chunks.text: Oral sources include folktales, folksongs, proverbs, ballads, and owis (Marathi verses in the oral tradition). Activists during the Sanyukta Maharashtra Movement were inspired by the powadas of Lokshahir Anna Bhau Sathe and Shahir Amar Sheikh. These sources preserve the history and spirit of social movements through spoken and sung traditions.

content_chunks.heading: Audio-Visual Sources and FTII
content_chunks.text: Audio-visual sources include television, films, and the internet. The Government of India started the Film and Television Institute of India (FTII) at Pune in 1960 to provide public education. An institute called Indian News Review has produced newsreels on important events in politics, social issues, art, sports, and culture. The Film and Television Institute of India has also produced documentaries on prominent social leaders and major contributions to the country. These news releases and documentaries are useful for studying the history of modern India.

content_chunks.heading: India: Events after 1960 - The 1960s
content_chunks.text: In the 1960s, several events had a huge impact on India. The regions of Goa, Diu, and Daman were freed from Portuguese rule. Tensions between India and China on the northern border culminated in the war of 1962 in the MacMahon line region. Prime Minister Pandit Jawaharlal Nehru led India until Pandit Jawaharlal Nehru passed away in 1964. Lal Bahadur Shastri succeeded Pandit Jawaharlal Nehru as Prime Minister. During Lal Bahadur Shastri's tenure, India and Pakistan went to war over the Kashmir issue in 1965. Lal Bahadur Shastri gave the slogan 'Jai Jawan Jai Kisan' to highlight the importance of soldiers and farmers. Lal Bahadur Shastri passed away at Tashkent in 1966.

content_chunks.heading: The Tenure of Indira Gandhi and the 1970s
content_chunks.text: Indira Gandhi became the Prime Minister of India in 1966. Indira Gandhi's decisions included the nationalisation of banks and cancelling the pensions of Princes. In 1971, a war between India and Pakistan led to the creation of Bangladesh. India successfully carried out an underground nuclear test at Pokharan in Rajasthan in 1974. In 1975, Sikkim joined the Indian federation. In 1975, the government declared a state of National Emergency based on constitutional provisions. During the Emergency, fundamental rights were suspended and the administration became disciplined, but human rights were restricted. The Emergency lasted until 1977, after which the Janata Party, led by Morarji Desai, came to power.

content_chunks.heading: The 1980s: Challenges and Rajiv Gandhi
content_chunks.text: In the 1980s, the Indian political system faced challenges like the demand for an independent Khalistan by Sikhs in Punjab. In 1984, the Indian army conducted Operation Blue Star in the Golden Temple at Amritsar to evict terrorists. Indira Gandhi was assassinated by Indira Gandhi's bodyguards in 1984. Rajiv Gandhi became the Prime Minister and initiated reforms in the economy and science and technology. Rajiv Gandhi tried to solve the issues of the Tamil minority in Sri Lanka but the efforts were in vain. In 1989, the Congress party was defeated, and Vishwanath Pratap Singh became the Prime Minister, contributing the policy of reservations for Other Backward Classes (OBC).

content_chunks.heading: The 1990s: Economic Reforms and Globalisation
content_chunks.text: The year 1991 was a turning point as the Soviet Russia disintegrated and the Cold War ended. In India, the government under Prime Minister P.V. Narasimha Rao initiated economic reforms known as economic liberalisation. These changes are also described as globalisation. Between 1996 and 1999, India saw several Prime Ministers including Atal Bihari Vajpayee, H.D. Devegowda, and Inder Kumar Gujral. In 1999, the National Democratic Alliance came to power with Atal Bihari Vajpayee as Prime Minister. India conducted nuclear tests in 1998. In 1999, India defeated Pakistan in the Kargil War over the Kashmir issue.

content_chunks.heading: Indian Economy and Social Changes
content_chunks.text: Right from independence, the Indian economy has aimed for modernisation, self-sufficiency, and social justice. The National Planning Commission was established to coordinate development through Five Year Plans. Dr. M.S. Swaminathan is known as the father of the Green Revolution (1965), which increased foodgrain production. Dr. Verghese Kurien led the co-operative dairy movement known as the White Revolution. In the social field, the Department of Women and Child Development was created in 1985. Laws like the Prohibition of Dowry Act and the Prevention of Atrocities Act (1989) were passed to ensure social justice. Globalisation and telecommunication revolutions, including mobile phones and the internet, have transformed the lifestyle of Indians.

content_chunks.heading: The Unrest in Punjab and the Khalistan Movement
content_chunks.text: The Akali Dal was the major political party in Punjab. In 1973, the Akali Dal passed the ‘Aanandpur Sahib Resolution’ making demands for Chandigarh to be part of Punjab, inclusion of Punjabi speaking parts from other states, and more autonomy for Punjab. In 1980, the movement for ‘Independent Khalistan’ took root in Punjab. Sant Jarnailsingh Bhindranwale became a staunch supporter of Khalistan. Bhindranwale was arrested in 1981 for the murder of Lala Jagatnarayan, and the situation in Punjab worsened. President’s rule was imposed in Punjab in 1983. Bhindranwale’s followers captured the Golden Temple and built a barricade of sandbags, turning the religious site into a fort-like structure.

content_chunks.heading: Operation Blue Star and Operation Black Thunder
content_chunks.text: Operation Blue Star was a military expedition undertaken to evict terrorists hiding in the Golden Temple. The task was entrusted to Major General Kuldeep Singh Brar. The operation started on the morning of 3rd June 1984 and ended on 6th June with the death of Bhindranwale and other terrorists. In 1986, another operation called ‘Operation Black Thunder’ was conducted against terrorists in the Golden Temple. These operations were crucial for establishing peace in Punjab and were a major challenge to Indian democracy.

content_chunks.heading: Issues Concerning North-east India: Mizoram and Nagaland
content_chunks.text: North-east India consists of eight states: Assam, Arunachal Pradesh, Manipur, Meghalaya, Mizoram, Nagaland, Sikkim, and Tripura. In 1954, the North-East Frontier Agency (NEFA) was formed. In Mizoram, Laldenga established the Mizo National Front (MNF) in 1961 and demanded an independent nation. Prime Minister Indira Gandhi suppressed the rebellion, and in 1985, Prime Minister Rajiv Gandhi reached an agreement giving Mizoram the status of a full-fledged State. In Nagaland, the Naga National Council (NNC) under Angami Zapu Fizo demanded an independent state. After several discussions, Nagaland was made a full-fledged State on 1st December 1963.

content_chunks.heading: Assam Agitation and Arunachal Pradesh
content_chunks.text: In 1983, an intense agitation was staged in Assam by the All Assam Students Union and Assam Ganasangram Parishad over the dominance of Bengali migrants. In 1985, Prime Minister Rajiv Gandhi and Assamese leader Prafullakumar Mohanto signed an accord to send illegal Bangladeshi citizens back. In 1986, Prafullakumar Mohanto became the Chief Minister of Assam. Arunachal Pradesh, formerly part of NEFA, was created in 1972 and gained the status of a constituent State on 20th February 1987.

content_chunks.heading: Naxalism: A Challenge to Internal Security
content_chunks.text: The Naxalite movement started at Naxalbari in West Bengal in 1967. The Naxalites organized small land-holding farmers and labourers to raise a voice against exploitation by landlords. Later, the movement strayed from its objectives and took recourse to terrorism to prevent government welfare policies from reaching the common people. The Naxalites established the People’s Liberation Guerilla Army (PLGA). The movement spread to Andhra Pradesh, Telangana, Chhattisgarh, Maharashtra, and Odisha, posing a serious challenge to India’s internal security.

content_chunks.heading: Communalism and Regionalism
content_chunks.text: Communalism emerges out of narrow religious pride and turns into bigotry when one considers their religion superior to others. Fanaticism is the base of communalism and makes people oblivious to national interest, leading to riots and destruction of public property. Regionalism means having excessive pride in one’s region to the point of feeling superior to others. Regionalism thrives on regional imbalance in development. While states like Maharashtra and Gujarat progressed, states like Odisha and Bihar remained undeveloped. Reducing this economic imbalance is the only way to tackle the evil of regionalism and strengthen national unity.

content_chunks.heading: Economic Development: The Mixed Economy Model
content_chunks.text: After independence, Prime Minister Pandit Nehru adopted the 'mixed economy' model for India's development. A mixed economy works in three parts: the Public Sector (industries under complete government control, like defence), the Private Sector (industries owned by private individuals but supervised by the government, like consumer goods), and the Joint Sector (industries owned by private individuals but run under government management). This system aims to bring together the good aspects of capitalism and socialism, focusing on national interest and long-term development.

content_chunks.heading: Five Year Plans and the Planning Commission
content_chunks.text: India established the Planning Commission in 1950 with Pandit Nehru as Chairman. The Commission formulated Five Year Plans based on the principle of proportionate distribution of resources. The general goals included increasing national income, rapid industrialisation, and achieving self-sufficiency in food grain production. The First Five Year Plan (1951-1956) focused on agriculture and social development. The Second Five Year Plan (1956-1961) aimed at industrialisation with projects like the Bhilai Steel Plant and the Sindri chemical fertilisers plant. The Third Five Year Plan (1961-1966) sought a balance between industry and agriculture.

content_chunks.heading: Nationalisation of Banks
content_chunks.text: During the tenures of Pandit Nehru and Lal Bahadur Shastri, banking was a private sector monopoly. To stop this and cover deficits in government schemes, the government nationalised the ‘Imperial Bank’ in 1955, converting it into the State Bank of India. On 19th July 1969, Prime Minister Indira Gandhi nationalised 14 major banks, including Allahabad Bank, Bank of Baroda, and Canara Bank. In 1980, six more banks were nationalised. This move was inspired by socialist ideas and aimed at bringing bank profits into the government treasury.

content_chunks.heading: The 20-Point Programme and Workers' Strike
content_chunks.text: Prime Minister Indira Gandhi announced the 20-point programme on 1st July 1975 to transform India into a developed nation. Provisions included land ceilings, minimum wages for workers, and improvements in the public distribution system. In the 1980s, unrest grew among Mumbai textile mill workers. In 1981, workers expected a 20% bonus, but the Rashtriya Mill Mazdoor Sangh agreed to a lower amount without consulting workers. Dr Datta Samant led a strike starting on 18th January 1982, involving two and a half lakh workers. The strike lasted for a year, leading to significant unemployment and the decline of the 'town of mills' known as Girangaon.

content_chunks.heading: New Economic Policy of 1991
content_chunks.text: In 1991, the Indian economy faced a crisis with 17% inflation and low foreign exchange reserves. Prime Minister P.V. Narasimha Rao and Finance Minister Dr Manmohan Singh implemented the New Economic Policy. Corrective measures included removing restrictions on foreign investments, restricting the license system to 18 industries, and opening the public sector to private investment. The Securities and Exchange Board of India (SEBI) was established in 1992 to control the share market. India became a member of the World Trade Organisation (WTO) in 1995, aiming for free global trade and the removal of discriminatory trade laws.

content_chunks.heading: Primary Education and Operation Blackboard
content_chunks.text: The first Census of independent India in 1951 revealed a literacy level of 17%. To improve this, the central government started 'Operation Blackboard' in 1988 to spread primary education and improve standards. The government provided funds for classrooms, toilets, and teaching aids. In 1994, the scheme expanded to include more teachers and classrooms, with priority for girls' schools and rural areas. The District Primary Education Programme (DPEP) was started in 1994 for the universalisation of primary education. The 'mid-day meal' scheme was launched in 1995 to provide proper nourishment for students and encourage attendance.

content_chunks.heading: Secondary Education and the Kothari Commission
content_chunks.text: After independence, Education Minister Maulana Abul Kalam Azad initiated fundamental changes. The Mudaliar Commission (1952-53) studied secondary education and teaching methodology. In 1964, the Kothari Commission, under Dr D. S. Kothari, recommended the 10+2+3 pattern for secondary, higher secondary, and university education. This system was implemented in 1972. The Commission also suggested a uniform national system of education and the inclusion of mother tongue, Hindi, and English. The National Policy on Education 1986 aimed to provide equal educational opportunities to all students in India through a common core curriculum.

content_chunks.heading: Higher Education and NCERT
content_chunks.text: In 1948, the Central Government appointed the University Education Commission under Dr Sarvapalli Radhakrishnan to coordinate university education and financial grants. The National Council of Educational Research and Training (NCERT) was established in Delhi in 1961 to help the Central Government with school education policy and curricula. State Councils of Educational Research and Training (SCERT) were established in all states. The Maharashtra State Bureau of Textbook Production and Curriculum Research (Balbharati) was established in 1967 to prepare textbooks for school children in multiple languages.

content_chunks.heading: IGNOU and Open Universities
content_chunks.text: The Indira Gandhi National Open University (IGNOU) was established in 1985 to make higher education accessible to every household. IGNOU provides concessions in eligibility criteria and age for admission. In 1990, IGNOU started an audio-visual distance education programme through Akashvani and Doordarshan. In Maharashtra, the Yashwantrao Chavan Maharashtra Mukta Vidyapeeth was established in Nashik in 1989 to provide open university education.

content_chunks.heading: Scientific Research: CSIR
keywords: Council of Scientific and Industrial Research
content_chunks.text: The Council of Scientific and Industrial Research (CSIR) was established in 1950 to promote scientific research for the benefit of the people. CSIR began research in fields like physics, chemistry, and pharmaceuticals. Achievements of CSIR include making ink for marking voters, medicines for malaria and tuberculosis, and using DNA fingerprinting for the first time in India. CSIR also developed an earthquake early warning system and prepared a digital encyclopaedia of Indian traditional knowledge available in eight international languages.

content_chunks.heading: Empowerment of Women: Manifestation of Woman Power
content_chunks.text: Women have made significant contributions in the post-independence period. In 1972, under the leadership of Mrinal Gore, women in Mumbai organized the 'laatne morcha' (rolling pin demonstration) to protest the scarcity and rising prices of essential commodities. The Chipko movement in 1973 showed constructive woman power when women, led by Gaura Devi, protected trees in the Himalayan foothills by encircling them. These movements demonstrated the united strength of women in addressing social and environmental issues.

content_chunks.heading: Anti-liquor Movement and International Women’s Year
content_chunks.text: In 1992, women in Andhra Pradesh started a movement against the drinking of arrack (locally produced alcohol) after the 'Sitama Katha' story created awareness. This movement led to a strict government policy against the sale of alcohol. The United Nations declared 1975 as International Women’s Year, focusing on peace, development, and equality. In 1975, the government of India constituted the Committee on the Status of Women under Dr Phulrenu Guha to conduct a comprehensive survey on the social position and education of women.

content_chunks.heading: Laws for Women and Awareness Against Dowry
content_chunks.text: Laws were enacted to protect women's rights, including the right to alimony and a share in father's property in 1952. The Dowry Prohibition Act of 1961 made giving or taking dowry a cognizable criminal offence. The Maternity Benefit Act (1961) gave women the right to leave from work during childbirth. Despite these laws, dowry deaths continued, leading to the Dowry Prohibition (Amendment) Act in 1984. Family Courts were established in 1984 to resolve domestic problems and marriage conflicts justly. The Sati (Prevention) Act was passed in 1988 following the case of Roop Kanwar.

content_chunks.heading: Reservation for Women and Constitutional Amendments
content_chunks.text: The 73rd and 74th amendments to the Indian Constitution provided for the reservation of one-third of seats for women in Gram Panchayats, Panchayat Samitis, Zilla Parishads, and municipalities. Maharashtra and 15 other states have increased this reservation to 50%. These provisions allow women to participate in the business of the community and exercise their political rights effectively.

content_chunks.heading: Upliftment of Weaker Sections: SC, ST, and Minorities
content_chunks.text: The Indian Constitution adopted values of freedom and equality, banning untouchability under the 17th paragraph. Scheduled Castes and Scheduled Tribes were given reservations in education and jobs to facilitate development. The Prevention of Atrocities Act was passed in 1989 to protect these groups. Nomadic and De-notified Tribes, previously declared 'criminal tribes' by the British, were given representation in government sectors. Minorities are protected through constitutional rights to preserve their language, culture, and traditions, including the right to set up separate educational institutions.

content_chunks.heading: Indian Atomic Energy Commission
content_chunks.text: India’s first Prime Minister Pandit Jawaharlal Nehru established the Indian Atomic Energy Commission on 10th August 1948 to nurture a scientific temper and bring about progress. Dr Homi Bhabha was appointed as the first Chairman of the Indian Atomic Energy Commission. The objectives of the Indian Atomic Energy Commission were to produce electricity from atomic energy, increase the yield of food grains, and develop nanotechnology. In 1956, the Department of Atomic Energy set up ‘Apsara’, which was a nuclear reactor functioning on atomic energy. In 1969, an atomic power station was set up at Tarapur, and later a Reactor Research Centre was established at Kalpakkam to use Thorium for generating power.

content_chunks.heading: Nuclear Tests at Pokharan
content_chunks.text: India successfully performed the first nuclear test at Pokharan in Rajasthan on 18th May 1974 for peaceful purposes. Prime Minister Indira Gandhi took the decision to carry out this nuclear explosion due to China’s nuclear capability and Pakistan’s efforts to acquire nuclear weapons. Pokharan was chosen because the site was far from human settlement and had no ground water reserves. On 11th May 1998, India carried out a second nuclear test to prove nuclear preparedness under Prime Minister Atal Bihari Vajpayee. This test included a Hydrogen Bomb. India declared that there would be no first use of nuclear weapons, though the USA imposed economic sanctions on India immediately after the tests.

content_chunks.heading: Missile Development and DRDO
content_chunks.text: The Defence Research and Development Organisation (DRDO) was established in 1958 to make India self-sufficient in defence equipment. Under the leadership of Dr A.P.J. Abdul Kalam, the Integrated Guided Missile Development Programme (IGMDP) was conceived. India successfully tested the surface-to-surface ballistic missile ‘Prithvi’ in 1988 and the missile ‘Agni’ in 1989. Other developments include the ‘Akash’ missile (1990) for land-to-air attacks and the ‘Nag’ anti-tank missile. Due to these contributions, Dr A.P.J. Abdul Kalam is known as the ‘Missile Man’ of India.

content_chunks.heading: Space Research and ISRO
content_chunks.text: Indian space research began in 1961 with the launch of a research rocket from Thumba. The Indian Space Research Organisation (ISRO) was established on 15th August 1969 with headquarters in Bengaluru. India launched its first satellite ‘Aryabhatta’ in 1975 with help from the Soviet Russia. Bhaskar-1 was launched in 1979 for remote sensing of the Earth's surface. APPLE (Ariane Passenger Payload Experiment) was the first completely indigenously built satellite, launched in 1981. The INSAT (Indian National Satellite) series, started in 1983, revolutionized telecommunication, television, and weather forecasting in India.

content_chunks.heading: Technological Progress in Communication and Transport
content_chunks.text: Communication technology advanced with the start of the Telex service in 1963 and the introduction of the six-digit Pincode system on 15th August 1972. International Subscriber Dialling (ISD) started in 1972 to connect Mumbai and London directly. Mobile phone services started in India on 22nd August 1994. In the transport sector, the Konkan Railway started in 1998, stretching 760 kilometres across Maharashtra, Goa, Karnataka, and Kerala. The Konkan Railway is noted for technological feats like the 6.5 km Karbude tunnel and the 64m tall Panval bridge. Computerised reservation systems for rail travel were first introduced in Delhi in 1984.

content_chunks.heading: Industry and Trade: Major Industries
content_chunks.text: After independence, the Industrial Finance Corporation of India (1948) and the Industrial Development Corporation (1954) were established to provide loans and develop the industrial sector. The textile industry forms 14% of total industrial production. The automobile industry is known as the ‘sunrise sector’ in India; India is a major producer of vehicles and the world's largest tractor manufacturer. The cement industry is vital for housing and infrastructure. Other significant industries include the silk industry, jute industry (where India is a top producer), and the leather industry, which is primarily export-oriented.

content_chunks.heading: Agriculture and Rural Industries
content_chunks.text: Agriculture is the major occupation in India, with 70% of society depending on jobs related to agricultural production. Traditional methods using bullocks are now supplemented by machines for ploughing and harvesting. Modern techniques like drip irrigation and organic farming are increasingly used. The Khadi and Village Industries Commission was established to promote industrialisation in rural areas and make villages self-reliant. The government provides loans to farmers through rural banks and organizes study visits through Panchayat Samitis to improve farming techniques.

content_chunks.heading: Mineral Wealth and Natural Resources
content_chunks.text: The availability of iron and coal is essential for industrial development. India has adequate stores of manganese and mineral oil. The Oil and Natural Gas Commission (ONGC) was established in 1956 to search for mineral oil and gas deposits. In 1974, the Oil and Natural Gas Commission started digging an oil well in the ‘Bombay High’ area using a drillship called ‘Sagar Samrat’. This area eventually contributed 38% of the total mineral oil production in India.

content_chunks.heading: Trade: Internal and Foreign
content_chunks.text: India’s foreign trade involves the import of machines, iron, mineral oil, and medicines, while exports include tea, coffee, spices, cotton textiles, and leather. Foreign trade is essential to gain valuable foreign exchange. Internal trade takes place via railways, waterways, roads, and airways. Major ports like Mumbai, Kolkata, Cochi, and Chennai are important for internal trade. Commodities like coal, cotton, rice, and wheat are major items in India's internal trade network.

content_chunks.heading: Modernisation and the Indian Constitution
content_chunks.text: Human life in India changed rapidly between 1961 and 2000 due to modernisation. Dr Babasaheb Ambedkar brought about a sea change in the traditional Indian mindset through the medium of the Indian Constitution. According to the Indian Constitution, all Indians are equal before the law and cannot be discriminated against on the basis of religion, race, caste, gender, or place of birth. These constitutional provisions shook the foundations of the caste system and helped make the practice of family-run vocations obsolete. In 1978, Railway Minister Madhu Dandawate put an end to the third-class compartments in railway trains, which had previously served as a symbol of the Indian social hierarchy.

content_chunks.heading: Social Welfare and Public Health
content_chunks.text: The government of India established the Ministry of Social Welfare on 14th June 1964 to implement programmes for nutrition, child development, and social security. In the medical field, Dr N. Gopinath performed the first successful open-heart surgery in India in 1962 at Christian Medical College Hospital in Vellore. Dr Pramod Sethi designed and manufactured the 'Jaipur Foot', which transformed the lives of the disabled by providing artificial limbs. The first successful kidney transplant in India was performed in 1971. India also achieved success in artificial conception with the birth of 'Durga' via test-tube baby technology in 1978. The 'pulse polio' immunisation programme, started in 1995, has successfully controlled polio in India.

content_chunks.heading: Rural Development and Urbanisation
content_chunks.text: Rural development was a major focus in the post-independence period. The collective development scheme empowered Gram Panchayats, Panchayat Samitis, and Zilla Parishads with more authority. Rural electrification was essential for development, providing power for automatic pumps and lights for studying. By 1973, three thousand villages in India were provided with electricity. Urbanisation is the process of population concentration in cities. Factors affecting urbanisation include industrialisation, reduction in mortality rates, and migration from rural areas in search of job opportunities. To reduce the strain on cities, the government aims to achieve a balance in economic development between urban and rural areas.

content_chunks.heading: Changing Life : 2 - Language and Sports
content_chunks.text: India has many official languages, including Hindi, Assamese, Bengali, and Marathi. While regional dialects are on the decline, Hindi movies have served to unite the country by language. English has gained ground due to globalisation, opening doors to multiple job opportunities. In the field of sports, Geet Sethi achieved global excellence in snooker. In 1983, the Indian cricket team won the World Cup under the captainship of Kapil Dev, which brought great popularity to the sport across the country. In 2000, Karnam Malleshwari became the first Indian woman to win an Olympic medal in weightlifting.

content_chunks.heading: Theatre, Films, and Media
content_chunks.text: Theatre and films are vital aspects of Indian life. The age of black and white movies was succeeded by coloured movies, and film shooting locales moved abroad. The concept of single-screen cinema halls has largely given way to multiplexes. Newspapers have evolved from black and white to colour printing, functioning to shape public opinion and keep a watch over government machinery. Television in India changed from limited black and white broadcasts to a wide range of coloured channels. In 1991, CNN showed live visual reporting of the Iraq war to the world. In 1998, STAR (Satellite Television Asia Region) entered India, leading to a sea change in Indian news telecasts.

content_chunks.heading: Post World War Political Developments: Interdependence
content_chunks.text: The international system is composed of many independent and sovereign states. Interdependence is a key feature of this system, as no nation can be completely self-sufficient regardless of its size or prosperity. Nations interact constantly and enter into treaties with each other. Every nation has a foreign policy to manage its internal dealings and its relations with other nations. The study of political science helps in understanding these complex international relationships and global issues.

content_chunks.heading: The World Wars and the League of Nations
content_chunks.text: The world was shaped by two major world wars in the 20th century. The First World War (1914-1918) caused tremendous loss of life and property. The League of Nations was established after the First World War to solve international disputes and avoid future wars. However, the League of Nations failed to prevent the rise of autocratic regimes in Germany and Italy, leading to the Second World War (1939-1945). The Second World War was more destructive and widespread, involving the use of nuclear weapons by America on Hiroshima and Nagasaki. The United Nations was established in 1945 to ensure global peace and security.

content_chunks.heading: The Cold War and Non-alignment
content_chunks.text: The Cold War (1945-1991) was a period of extreme rivalry between America and the Soviet Russia. Although there was no open war, tensions arose from an arms race and ideological differences. This led to the bipolarisation of the world, where nations joined either the American bloc or the Soviet bloc. Military organisations like NATO (led by America) and the Warsaw Pact (led by Soviet Russia) were created. The Non-aligned Movement (NAM) emerged as some countries, led by India's Prime Minister Pandit Nehru and other world leaders, decided to stay out of the super power rivalry to maintain their independence and promote global peace.

content_chunks.heading: End of the Cold War and Globalisation
content_chunks.text: The Cold War ended in 1991 with the disintegration of the Soviet Russia. Mikhail Gorbachev, the President of the Soviet Russia, implemented policies of Perestroika (Restructuring) and Glasnost (Openness), which gave impetus to democracy. After the Cold War, America remained the only super power. World politics shifted from political and military relations to economic and trade relations. Globalisation emerged as a process where capital, labour, markets, and information began circulating globally, making the boundaries between nations less sacrosanct. While globalisation brought benefits, it also increased the gap between rich and poor nations.

content_chunks.heading: Meaning and Importance of Foreign Policy
content_chunks.text: Foreign policy is the theoretical framework in which a sovereign and independent country takes decisions regarding international relations. National interest and foreign policy are closely connected, as national interest is fostered through foreign policy. National interest implies the means through which the independence and sovereignty of a country are safeguarded. Defence and economic development are the two most important components of national interest. Foreign policy is dynamic and changes according to conditions and time, though some principles remain eternal.

content_chunks.heading: Factors Influencing Foreign Policy
content_chunks.text: Several factors affect the decision-making process of foreign policy. Geographical location, including the size, population, and coastline of a nation, must be considered. In a democratic political system, the nation’s parliament plays a major role in evolving foreign policy. The economic condition of a country impacts foreign policy by shaping the need for trade relations and economic security. National leadership, including the Prime Minister and Foreign Minister, maintains continuity in policy. Administrative factors involve the Ministry of External Affairs and the National Security Advisor, who collect information and provide advice to shape the final decisions.

content_chunks.heading: India’s Foreign Policy: Principles and Stages
content_chunks.text: India’s foreign policy is guided by Article 51 of the Directive Principles of State Policy, which emphasizes international peace, security, and respect for international law. In the early phase (1947-1990), Prime Minister Pandit Nehru shaped the policy around non-alignment and self-reliance. In the second stage (1991 onwards), India adopted a free market policy and dynamic international participation, including the 'Look East' and 'Act East' policies. India’s nuclear policy aimed at both electricity generation and military capability, leading to nuclear tests in 1974 and 1998. India remains a responsible nuclear power committed to disarmament and global peace.

content_chunks.heading: India’s Defence System: National Security
content_chunks.text: National security involves keeping the security systems well-prepared to defend against foreign aggression and internal unrest. India’s security system includes the Army (protecting geographical boundaries), the Navy (protecting the coastline), and the Air Force (protecting air space). The President of India is the supreme commander of the defence forces, representing civil power. Paramilitary forces like the Border Security Force (BSF), Coast Guard, Central Reserve Police Force (CRPF), and Rapid Action Force (RAF) assist the main forces in maintaining law and order and managing disasters.

content_chunks.heading: Challenges to Security and Human Security
content_chunks.text: India faces external threats from Pakistan and China, involving border disputes and infiltrations. Internal security is threatened by regionalism, rebellious movements like the Naxalite movement, and terrorism. In the post-Cold War period, the concept of security broadened to include human security. Human security refers to human-centric thinking where individuals are protected from dangers like illiteracy, poverty, and contagious diseases. Protecting human rights and creating a conducive environment for a respectable life are essential components of human security.

content_chunks.heading: The United Nations (UN)
content_chunks.text: The United Nations was established on 24th October 1945 to ensure global peace and security. The United Nations Charter describes the structure and functioning of the organization. The main organs include the General Assembly (a platform for all member nations), the Security Council (responsible for international peace), the Economic and Social Council, the International Court of Justice (ICJ), and the Secretariat. The Security Council has five permanent members with veto power: America, Russia, England, France, and China. India has played a major role in the United Nations, particularly in peacekeeping forces and raising issues like decolonisation and racism.

content_chunks.heading: India and other Countries
content_chunks.text: India maintains relations with neighbouring countries like Pakistan, China, Bangladesh, and Sri Lanka based on equality and mutual respect. Relations with Pakistan are influenced by the Kashmir issue and terrorism. Sino-Indian relations involve border disputes in the Aksai Chin and Macmahon line areas. India established the South Asian Association for Regional Cooperation (SAARC) in 1985 to encourage economic cooperation in the region. India also has strong ties with America (Civil Nuclear Agreement), Russia (defence and technology), and European nations. Cooperation with Africa and the Indo-Pacific area focuses on trade, security, and development.

content_chunks.heading: International Problems: Human Rights and Environment
content_chunks.text: International problems affect all countries and require joint efforts to solve. Human rights are natural rights necessary for living as a human being, proclaimed by the Universal Declaration of Human Rights in 1948. Environmental degradation, including global warming and species extinction, is a global dimension addressed in conferences from Stockholm (1972) to Paris (2015). Terrorism is a major global problem that uses violence to achieve political goals and threatens internal security. Refugees are people forced to leave their motherland due to persecution or war, facing challenges in resettlement and placing a strain on the host nation's economy.
